metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the custom-verifier registration contract in the plan (e.g., 'mcp.auth_strategies may include a dotted Python import path "module:ClassName" for a class implementing TokenVerifier; the composite resolves it at startup via importlib').
findings.finding: The 'pluggable custom' verifier mode is listed as one of the five required verifier strategies (named in Scope, AC3, and AC4) but the plan does not specify HOW an operator registers a custom class via the flat `mcp.auth_strategies` string list — e.g., whether a dotted Python import path ('myorg.auth:MyVerifier') is used, an entry-point group, a factory function, or some other mechanism. Without this, an implementing agent faces a design fork that could produce an interface incompatible with the docs and AC.
findings.location: Scope section ('pluggable custom') and AC: 'All five verifier modes (static, JWKS/JWT, introspection, trusted-proxy, custom) are selectable via the flat mcp.auth_strategies list'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Scope section: 'The custom verifier slot is registered by supplying a dotted import path ("module.path:ClassName") in mcp.auth_strategies; the composite resolves it at startup via importlib.import_module and validates that the class implements TokenVerifier before the server accepts connections.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `required_scopes` (and any other new auth-related flat config keys beyond the four already named) with their canonical env-var names, types, and defaults in the plan's Compatibility & Rollback or Scope section so AC6 is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The `required_scopes` server-level config key is referenced in the Security section and Success Criteria ('server-level required_scopes') as a first-class admission control mechanism, but it is not listed among the named flat config keys in the Compatibility/Rollback section, MCP_ENV_VARS reference, or AC6. An implementing agent cannot ensure the key appears in server.json and env-vars.md without knowing its canonical name and type.
findings.location: Security section ('server-level required_scopes and the existing REBAR_MCP_READONLY/ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC/ALLOW_LLM env gates') and AC6 ('New flat config keys and env vars are reflected in MCP_ENV_VARS and server.json')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Compatibility & Rollback: 'New env vars: REBAR_MCP_TRANSPORT (str, default "stdio"), REBAR_MCP_AUTH_ENABLED (bool, default false), REBAR_MCP_AUTH_RESOURCE_SERVER_URL (str), REBAR_MCP_AUTH_STRATEGIES (comma-separated, default ""), REBAR_MCP_REQUIRED_SCOPES (space-separated OAuth scopes, default ""), REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP (bool, default false).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion explicitly verifying (a) that no bearer token is forwarded to any upstream call (confused-deputy prohibition) and (b) that tokens/secrets are redacted from logs, with passing tests for both.
findings.finding: The Security section describes two concrete, testable deliverables — 'no token passthrough to any upstream (confused-deputy prohibition)' and 'tokens/secrets never logged (a redaction layer is required and tested)' — but no Acceptance Criterion covers either. These are orphan deliverables.
findings.location: Security section ('No token passthrough to any upstream…; tokens/secrets never logged (a redaction layer is required and tested)') vs Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC bullet such as: 'Bearer tokens and secrets are never forwarded to upstream calls (tested by asserting outbound HTTP fixtures contain no Authorization header) and are redacted from structured logs (tested by asserting the token string is absent from captured log output).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add non-loopback-without-allowlists (and non-loopback without TLS-at-edge ack) to the AC fail-closed startup list so it matches the Success Criteria guarantee.
findings.finding: The Success Criteria section states that a non-loopback bind must refuse to start unless explicit host/origin allowlists AND a TLS-at-edge acknowledgment are present, but the Acceptance Criteria fail-closed startup list omits this case, creating an observable gap between the two sections.
findings.location: Success Criteria ('non-loopback bind requires explicit host/origin allowlists AND an explicit TLS-at-edge acknowledgment') vs Acceptance Criteria AC line 5 (fail-closed startup list)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC line 5: '…and a non-loopback `auth_bind_host` without both `auth_allowed_hosts`/`auth_allowed_origins` and `REBAR_MCP_TLS_AT_EDGE_ACK` all refuse to start.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the ADR deliverable description (AC docs bullet) that the new ADR supersedes or amends ADR-0007, and add 'update or mark ADR-0007 as partially superseded' to the doc deliverables list.
findings.finding: ADR-0007, which documents the deliberate stdio-only architecture (remote needs met via FastAPI, not MCP-over-HTTP), is cited as the existing design-of-record but is not listed as being superseded, amended, or cross-referenced by the new ADR this epic produces. This risks leaving a stale authoritative architectural reference that contradicts the new optional HTTP transport capability.
findings.location: Context section ('per ADR-0007'); Acceptance Criteria — docs bullet (`docs/mcp-auth.md` and ADR deliverables)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Acceptance Criteria docs bullet, change 'an ADR document the resource-server-only model…' to 'an ADR (superseding/amending ADR-0007) document the resource-server-only model…', and add a task to annotate ADR-0007 with a 'Superseded-in-part by ADR-XXXX' header so the corpus remains internally consistent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a gating proof-of-concept in S1 (or as a pre-S1 spike) that imports and exercises `AuthSettings`, `TransportSecuritySettings`, `mcp.streamable_http_app()`, and `mcp.session_manager.run()` from the pinned `mcp>=1.28.1` package, confirming the constructor signatures and import paths the plan assumes — so any API divergence surfaces before verifier code is written.
findings.finding: The plan's entire auth-wiring mechanism depends on four SDK API shapes — `FastMCP(token_verifier=…, auth=AuthSettings(…))`, `TransportSecuritySettings`, `mcp.streamable_http_app()`, and `mcp.session_manager.run()` — that only exist at `mcp>=1.28.1`. The plan correctly names the version pin but does not instruct implementers to verify these APIs (import path, constructor signatures) against the pinned version before building the verifier framework. The currently-installed SDK (pre-pin) does not expose `AuthSettings` or `TransportSecuritySettings` through any resolvable path, meaning five stories of verifier work could be built against an assumed API shape that is wrong, with the mismatch discovered only at integration time in S1 or S2.
findings.location: Approach & rationale (positive) / Compatibility & rollback
findings.suggested_fix: In S1's scope or as a named prerequisite, add a single smoke-test module (e.g., `tests/test_sdk_api_contract.py`) that imports each of the four SDK symbols from their expected paths and instantiates them with the expected kwargs (or inspects their signatures). This converts an implicit API assumption into an explicit, CI-enforced contract check that runs before S2 begins.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before S1 begins, confirm that `TransportSecuritySettings` (or its equivalent) exists in the actual `mcp>=1.28.1` published release (check the mcp changelog/GitHub tags or install `mcp==1.28.1` and run `resolve_symbol`) and document the correct import path and field names in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan's primary transport-hardening mechanism — `TransportSecuritySettings` (used for DNS-rebinding and Origin protection via `FastMCP(..., transport_security=TransportSecuritySettings(...))`) — is asserted to exist in `mcp>=1.28.1` but is UNRESOLVED in the installed `mcp>=1.9` environment across every plausible module path (`mcp`, `mcp.server.fastmcp`, `mcp.server.auth.settings`). The plan pins `mcp>=1.28.1,<2` for this symbol but never verifies it actually exists in that version range's published API surface. If the symbol is absent or has a different name/location in 1.28.x, the entire DNS-rebinding/Origin-protection model in the Security section must be re-implemented manually.
findings.location: Security section ('Transport hardening: DNS-rebinding/Origin protection on by default'); Compatibility & rollback ('pinning mcp>=1.28.1,<2')
findings.suggested_fix: Install `mcp==1.28.1` in a scratch env and run `python -c 'from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP; help(FastMCP.__init__)'` plus `python -c 'import mcp; print(dir(mcp))'` to confirm the symbol and its import path. If absent, replace the `TransportSecuritySettings` references with an explicit Starlette middleware plan (e.g., a custom `TrustedHostMiddleware`/`CORSMiddleware` wrapping the ASGI app).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact name and import path of the Streamable-HTTP ASGI entrypoint in `mcp==1.28.1` (e.g., check the mcp SDK changelog, the `simple-auth` example, or install the target version), and update the Testing Strategy section with the confirmed symbol name and import.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `streamable_http_app()` is available as the ASGI entrypoint from the official SDK (used for integration testing via `httpx ASGITransport`), but this symbol is UNRESOLVED in the installed environment across both `mcp` and `mcp.server.fastmcp`. Like `TransportSecuritySettings`, this is gated on the `mcp>=1.28.1` pin — but is never verified to exist in that version's public API. If the ASGI app is exposed differently in 1.28.x (e.g., via `FastMCP.asgi_app` attribute, `FastMCP.streamable_http()`, or an ASGI factory with a different name), the integration-test strategy named in the Testing Strategy section will not compile.
findings.location: Testing strategy section ('driving the SDK's Streamable-HTTP ASGI entrypoint with an in-process `httpx` `ASGITransport` client'); Approach & rationale

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before S1 begins, empirically verify the exact constructor signatures of `AuthSettings`, `TransportSecuritySettings`, and `FastMCP` (specifically the `token_verifier=`, `auth=`, `host=`, `port=`, `transport_security=` kwargs) against `mcp==1.28.1` (or whichever release in `>=1.28.1,<2` will be pinned), and record the verified field names in the plan/ADR so child stories implement against confirmed surfaces.
findings.finding: `AuthSettings`, `TransportSecuritySettings`, and the `FastMCP(token_verifier=…, auth=AuthSettings(…))` constructor kwargs are PARTIALLY-VERIFIED: they are central to the plan's wiring but cannot be confirmed at flag level in the installed `mcp>=1.9` environment. The plan targets `mcp>=1.28.1`, which is 19 minor versions above the current lower bound. If the field names on `AuthSettings` (e.g. `issuer_url`, `resource_server_url`, `required_scopes`) or `TransportSecuritySettings` (e.g. `enable_dns_rebinding_protection`, `allowed_hosts`, `allowed_origins`) differ from what the plan asserts, or if `FastMCP` does not accept `token_verifier=` as a constructor kwarg, the entire auth wiring and transport-security posture fails silently at runtime rather than at plan-review time.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — `FastMCP(token_verifier=…, auth=AuthSettings(…))` wiring; Security — `TransportSecuritySettings`; Compatibility — `mcp>=1.28.1,<2`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre-S1 spike task: `pip install 'mcp==1.28.1'` (or the target release) in a scratch env, then `python -c "from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP; import inspect; print(inspect.signature(FastMCP.__init__))"` and equivalently for `AuthSettings` and `TransportSecuritySettings`. Record the verified kwarg names. Update the plan's wiring snippets to match. This is a one-hour verification that de-risks the entire epic's security wiring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the exact import path and calling convention for obtaining a Streamable-HTTP ASGI app object from `FastMCP` in `mcp>=1.28.1` (e.g., `FastMCP.streamable_http_app()`, `FastMCP.get_asgi_app()`, or a property), and specify this in the Testing Strategy section so child stories implement against the confirmed interface.
findings.finding: The plan uses `streamable_http_app()` (or an equivalent ASGI accessor on `FastMCP`) as the integration-test entrypoint to drive the SDK's HTTP transport via `httpx.ASGITransport`, but neither the exact import path nor the function's existence in `mcp>=1.28.1` is confirmed — it is UNRESOLVED in the installed environment and no module path is specified in the plan text.
findings.location: Testing strategy — 'driving the SDK's Streamable-HTTP ASGI entrypoint with an in-process `httpx` `ASGITransport` client'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access class of the `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP` + non-loopback combination and state explicitly whether the TLS-at-edge acknowledgment requirement also applies to auth-disabled non-loopback binds (and add a corresponding startup-refuse-to-start AC if so).
findings.finding: The unauthenticated-HTTP + non-loopback combination lacks a declared access class and an explicit TLS-at-edge requirement. The plan states non-loopback binds require TLS-at-edge ack (for auth-enabled paths) and separately that HTTP with auth disabled is allowed with REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP. But the intersection — auth disabled AND non-loopback host — is never given an access-class declaration or a stated requirement that TLS-at-edge still applies. A plaintext, unauthenticated, publicly-bound HTTP endpoint carrying full rebar tool access is the worst-case outcome of this ambiguity.
findings.location: Security section — 'Transport hardening' and 'Fail-closed everywhere'; also AC5 (startup refuse-to-start list)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC line: 'A non-loopback bind with auth disabled (REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP set) ALSO requires the TLS-at-edge ack; without it the server refuses to start.' Declare the surface access class in docs/mcp-auth.md as 'internal-only / operator-must-ensure-network-isolation'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access posture of the default-empty `required_scopes` configuration (e.g., 'public-access to all tools — operator must set required_scopes to restrict') and add either a startup warning when auth is enabled but required_scopes is empty, or document a minimum recommended scope in docs/mcp-auth.md.
findings.finding: The default posture when `required_scopes` is omitted grants every authenticated remote actor rebar's full ambient authority (write tools, reconcile live, auto-push, Jira creds) with no declared access level for this case. The plan states required_scopes is the only server-level authz gate but never declares what the empty/absent-scopes default means for destructive tools on the network-reachable path — an undeclared access posture on a boundary-crossing sensitive path.
findings.location: Security section — 'Authenticated ≠ authorized (named residual)'; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'When auth_enabled=true and required_scopes=[] (the default), any validated token is granted full tool access — operators deploying write-capable or reconcile-live-capable endpoints SHOULD set required_scopes to at minimum a dedicated rebar-mcp audience scope.' Add a startup INFO-level log when auth is enabled with empty required_scopes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC test oracle for the verifier-exception fail-closed path: 'a verifier that raises an exception must produce a 401 (not 500 or acceptance) at the composite, tested by injecting a raising stub verifier'.
findings.finding: The 'fail-closed: verifier raises → non-acceptance' invariant is asserted in the Security section prose but is absent from the AC test oracle. AC3 enumerates 401/PRM/200/403/audience-mismatch test cases but omits the exception-path case (a verifier that raises an exception must produce a 401, not a 500 or an acceptance). This is a boundary-crossing security invariant whose correctness is unverifiable from the named AC tests alone.
findings.location: Security section — 'Fail-closed everywhere'; AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the closed string-token vocabulary for `auth_strategies` (e.g., `static | jwt | introspection | proxy | custom`) directly in the plan's Scope or a dedicated config-schema section, so the factory dispatch table, the fail-closed unknown-entry guard, and operator docs all derive from a single canonical list.
findings.finding: The `auth_strategies` config field is the central enum selector for the composite verifier, and the fail-closed rule ('unknown auth_strategies entry refuses to start') depends entirely on knowing the valid token vocabulary — but the plan never defines what string values an operator may write (e.g., whether the JWT verifier is selected via 'jwt', 'jwks', 'jwks_jwt', 'oidc', etc.). The five verifier types are named only in prose ('static bearer, JWKS/JWT, RFC 7662 introspection, trusted-proxy passthrough, pluggable custom'); the corresponding machine-readable string tokens are absent from the plan.
findings.location: Scope section ('composite TokenVerifier … + config-driven selection … five verifiers'); AC line 'All five verifier modes (static, JWKS/JWT, introspection, trusted-proxy, custom) are selectable via the flat mcp.auth_strategies list'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a small table to the Scope section: `auth_strategies` accepts a comma-separated list of: `static` (StaticBearerVerifier), `jwt` (JWKSVerifier), `introspection` (RFC7662Verifier), `proxy` (TrustedProxyVerifier), `custom` (operator-supplied CustomVerifier). Any other token is an unknown strategy and causes startup refusal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the registration protocol for the 'pluggable custom' verifier mode: define what config key(s) an operator sets (e.g., `mcp.auth_custom_verifier` as a dotted import path), how the composite loads and instantiates the class, and what interface it must implement (i.e., `TokenVerifier.verify_token`).
findings.finding: The 'pluggable custom' verifier is named as one of the five required shipped verifier modes (Scope, AC3/AC4) and must be 'selectable via the flat mcp.auth_strategies list', but the plan nowhere specifies the registration mechanism — how does a `custom` strategy token cause the composite to load an operator-supplied `TokenVerifier` implementation? The plan does not state whether registration is via a dotted Python import path config key, a Python entry-point, a subclass discovery pattern, or some other mechanism.
findings.location: Scope section ('pluggable custom'); AC line 'All five verifier modes … custom … are selectable via the flat mcp.auth_strategies list'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] S5 must specify the concrete mechanism (e.g., contextvars keyed to the request, a sentinel token string, or a custom SDK hook) by which the ASGI middleware communicates validated proxy identity to the verify_token component, and validate that this mechanism is compatible with mcp>=1.28.1 SDK internals.
findings.finding: S5's TrustedProxyVerifier architecture is incompatible with the SDK's TokenVerifier.verify_token(token: str) seam: the seam receives only a bearer token string, but the proxy verifier needs validated HTTP header state (proxy identity) injected by the ASGI middleware — the communication mechanism between the middleware and the verifier component is unspecified.
findings.location: child 85a6-2fea-e2c9-4819: S5 — Trusted-proxy passthrough verifier, 'What' section and Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Use a request-scoped contextvars.ContextVar set by the middleware before the MCP app processes the request; the TrustedProxyVerifier.verify_token reads from the ContextVar (ignoring the token string argument). Document that this verifier only accepts calls from within the middleware-wrapped ASGI context. Confirm the SDK's BearerAuthBackend calls verify_token within the same async context as the middleware.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether each story's 'regenerate' step produces the full derived file (requires strict sequential merge) or appends to a shared file (requires a merge strategy); alternatively, designate a single integration step (S7 or a dedicated merge commit) as the sole regeneration point and have S1–S6 only register keys in the source-of-truth data file.
findings.finding: The per-story 'regenerates its slice' model for generated files (MCP_ENV_VARS, server.json, docs/mcp-reference.md, docs/env-vars.md) is underspecified: it is ambiguous whether each story regenerates the full file or patches a slice, creating an unresolvable merge conflict risk on generated artifacts when S1–S6 are developed in parallel branches.
findings.location: children 37dc-0a2d-8907-442d (S1), 61e6-6a65-f162-47cf (S2), 94c4-c391-3fed-4773 (S3), c9d8-eeb2-67a4-42fd (S4), 85a6-2fea-e2c9-4819 (S5), f978-847d-f874-4f2d (S6) — all 'registers its keys in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json + regenerates docs' clauses; child 4c7f-4fd3-11ce-474c (S7) final consistency check

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either move the 'auth_custom_import executes code at load' documentation requirement into S6's own AC (as a docs/comment in the code or a README note) so S6 is self-contained, or explicitly add S7 to S6's dependency list.
findings.finding: S6 has an acceptance criterion item that is externally gated on S7's deliverable ('docs/mcp-auth.md (S7) documents that auth_custom_import is a trusted operator config value that executes code at load'), creating an undeclared forward dependency from S6 to S7 not present in the stated dependency graph.
findings.location: child f978-847d-f874-4f2d: S6 — Pluggable custom verifier, Acceptance Criteria item 6
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S6's AC: 'A code comment in _mcp_auth.py (or a note in the module docstring) warns that auth_custom_import executes operator-configured code at startup, and S7 expands on this in docs/mcp-auth.md.' This makes S6 self-contained on the security warning while allowing S7 to elaborate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to S5's integration test AC an explicit oracle check: unauthenticated request (no valid proxy secret) → 401 AND WWW-Authenticate header contains the RFC 9728 resource_metadata URL.
findings.finding: S5's integration test asserts only 'a bare request → 401' without verifying the WWW-Authenticate header carries the RFC 9728 resource_metadata URL, while the parent AC requires this header check for all unauthenticated requests, creating a scope-narrowing partial coverage for the proxy verifier path.
findings.location: child 85a6-2fea-e2c9-4819: S5 — Trusted-proxy passthrough verifier, Acceptance Criteria integration test items
findings.suggested_fix: In S5's integration test, assert: `response.status_code == 401` AND `'resource_metadata' in response.headers.get('www-authenticate', '')`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to S1's ACs (or an explicit feature-flag guard in the code) that the HTTP transport, when auth_enabled is stubbed to false, should emit a prominent startup warning indicating auth is not yet wired, beyond just requiring the allow_unauthenticated_http ack.
findings.finding: S1 opens a real network listener with HTTP transport while treating auth_enabled as unconditionally false (stub), creating a window between S1 merging and S2 merging where an operator can deploy an authenticated-transport-less HTTP endpoint by setting only allow_unauthenticated_http=true, with no acceptance criterion in S1 restricting this to development/branch use only.
findings.location: child 37dc-0a2d-8907-442d: S1 — Optional Streamable-HTTP transport, 'What' section ('Auth config lands in S2; until then auth enabled is treated as false') and Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: In S1's implementation, when transport=http and auth_enabled is the stub-false value, emit a WARN log: 'HTTP transport started without authentication framework (S2 not yet wired); all requests will be unauthenticated. Set REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP=true to confirm this is intentional.' This is additive to the existing ack gate.

findings.finding: The plan introduces a verifier-mode selector (the `[mcp.auth]` config namespace) and names five modes — 'static bearer', 'JWKS/JWT', 'RFC 7662 introspection', 'trusted-proxy passthrough', 'pluggable custom' — but never defines the string vocabulary (the allowed values of the config key that selects among them). An implementor writing `McpConfig` extension, `MCP_ENV_VARS` entries, and the composite dispatcher needs a canonical enum such as `{"static", "jwks", "introspection", "proxy", "custom"}` (or equivalent); without it, S1–S5 child stories will each invent their own token strings and diverge.
findings.location: Scope section ('five verifiers … selectable via `[mcp.auth]` config / `REBAR_MCP_*` env vars'); AC line 4 ('all five verifier modes … selectable via `[mcp.auth]` config / `REBAR_MCP_*` env vars')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Config schema' subsection to Scope or Approach naming the selector key and its allowed string values, e.g.: `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_MODE ∈ {static, jwks, introspection, proxy, custom}` mapping to each verifier. This becomes the ground truth for `McpConfig` extension, `MCP_ENV_VARS`, and the composite dispatcher.

findings.finding: The parent AC and success criteria require 'wrong-audience rejection' to be demonstrated in the local test suite at the same observable level (HTTP status) as the co-listed guarantees (401-without-token, 200-with-valid-token, 403-insufficient-scope, DNS-rebinding 421/403). Across all seven children, wrong-audience rejection is only tested at the unit level (S2: 'unit test asserts... a wrong-audience token is rejected (returns None)'; S3: unit test 'rejection for wrong aud'). No child schedules an integration-level (httpx ASGITransport) assertion that a wrong-audience bearer token produces an HTTP 401 end-to-end through the transport. This is a scope-narrowing partial coverage: the child tests a precursor (the verifier returns None) but not the same observable outcome (HTTP 401 from the live endpoint).
findings.location: child 61e6-6a65-f162-47cf (S2); roster-wide absence confirmed across all siblings
findings.suggested_fix: In S2's integration test block, add a test case: set up the server with static auth enabled (resource_server_url='https://rebar.example.com'), issue a static token configured with aud='https://other.example.com', assert HTTP 401 in response. This mirrors the pattern of the existing 403 (wrong-scope) integration test.

findings.finding: S6's fourth acceptance criterion creates an undeclared ordering dependency on S7: 'docs/mcp-auth.md (in S7) documents that the import path is a trusted operator config value.' This makes S6 formally incomplete until S7 is delivered. However, S6's Scope section only declares 'Depends on S2. Independent of S3/S4/S5' — S7 is not listed as a dependency or blocker. A reviewer checking S6's own AC checklist cannot mark S6 done until S7 merges, but the dependency is invisible in the story's stated relationships, risking premature sign-off on S6.
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) add 'Completion of AC4 depends on S7' to S6's Scope section, or (b) move S6's AC4 into S7's acceptance criteria as 'docs/mcp-auth.md documents that the custom-verifier import path is a trusted operator config value, not derived from user input.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
